title: Why Orbán's Grip on Hungary Is Slipping

transcript.text:
There is an election in 
Hungary on April the 12th.

Viktor Orban is behind by double-digit
percentage points.
For more than a decade and a half,

Viktor Orban has outlasted
almost everyone in European politics.

He's proven very adept at 
holding on to power,

and he clearly has a great 
appetite for power.

But he and the Hungary he shaped
are under pressure from stagnant
growth, high inflation, mounting scandals
and now a formidable opponent.
Tisza is surging.
Look around you.

Hungary’s election is not just important
for voters in Hungary,

but also in Europe,
the US, as well as in Russia.

Whoever is in power,
they will have a very hard time over

the coming years to address both societal,
economic and political concerns.

And what happens in Hungary
won't stay in Hungary.

Inside Europe,
it wields influence far beyond its size.

The loan remains blocked because
one leader is not honoring his word.

So how did Orban create one of Europe's
most durable political machines?
Why is it now under strain?
And no matter who wins in April.

Why does Hungary's
next move affect the rest of the world?
As a member of the European Union,

even small countries can veto decisions
of global significance.
And Hungary is one of them.

Hungary's allies in both
the EU and NATO have become used to Orban
opposing many of their joint initiatives.

Hungary plans to block every EU decision
concerning Ukraine

until oil starts flowing again
through the Druzhba pipeline in Ukraine.

Hungary has dragged its feet
at NATO's expansion when it took in,
eventually Finland, as well as Sweden,

but only after serious
foot-dragging from Orban.

Very often it seems that the interest
that the Hungarian government

is trying to protect is that of Russia,
and sometimes maybe even China,

including via doubling down on 
Russian energy contracts

at a time when the EU is trying
to tighten sanctions.

Much of that stance reflects
the priorities of one man, Prime Minister

Viktor Orban, and his desire to move past
the country's former Soviet occupation.

He has cultivated ties in Moscow,
in Washington and Beijing.

He also waded into US
politics and endorsed

Donald Trump
during his first presidential campaign.

And Trump returned
the favor recently, endorsing Orban.
But that relationship is rooted

in a system the prime minister began
building years earlier.

Orban first won office in 1998 as
the leader of the right-wing Fidesz party.
He lost office in 2002, but not forever.
Orban returned to power after eight years
in opposition in 2010.

He came back to power with a mission
to institute regime change.

And to build what he later
called an illiberal state,

an ideology that stems from his disdain
of liberal democracy.

Almost the very first thing he did was to
pass a law changing the electoral system.

In effect, critics say, helping
entrench Fidesz’s advantage.
And that was only the start.
Orban also pushed through a new constitution.
This included removing effectively,
the top Supreme Court justice.
He also managed to install his political

loyalists in state institutions
as well as the media.
Newspapers were taken over by his business allies.
Some they actually just shut down.

That allowed him to essentially create
a national echo chamber.
Public media functions as a megaphone

communication channel
for the governing party.
It has that kind of sense of constantly

cultivating images of an enemy,
a threat from outside.
For years, that grip helped him control

the political story
even as economic strains mounted.
After returning to power

on a wave of anger
over the financial crisis, Orban

presided over years of modest growth,
rising wages, and new factory jobs.

His government delivered tangible
financial benefits to families.
It's just that with a sputtering economy,

these have become increasingly costly
to sustain.

Economic growth and inflation
have been really
the twin problems of the Orban government.

Hungary had the highest levels
of inflation during after the pandemic,

and Hungarians had been really struggling
to make ends meet
over the past several years.

Adding to the pressure, since 2015, house
prices in Hungary have more than tripled,

outpacing the EU average and rising faster
than incomes for many Hungarians.

It's a far cry from the tailwinds
Orban enjoyed upon his return to office.
Following the initial challenge

of overcoming the effects of the global
financial crisis.
He was very lucky at the start.

The world entered a period of low
inflation and global growth,

and then loads of European
Union money flooded in.

And critics say that actually a lot of that
luck was wasted

because it was plowed into kind of visible
projects like, you know, beautification

of town squares and prestige building
and things like that.

Meanwhile, neighbors like Poland
pulled ahead as Hungary lost momentum.

Whether it's income per
capita or household spending or cancer

survival rates or number of universities
in the global top thousand,

it's sort of
now kind of at the back of the queue,
whereas once it was at the top.

Hungary was doing relatively
well after its accession to the EU.

But if we look at the trajectory,
we see that the Hungarian economy

is lagging behind,
whereas Poland is taking off.
The government has had

to retrench investments,
including in public services

that were never really well
funded to begin with.

Orban himself
has had to pass an executive order
to stock hospitals with toilet paper.
Now, big industrial

bets were meant to restart growth
and fix a lot of that.
But so far they've fallen short.

Orban has sought to position
Hungary as a bridge between east and west,

including economically,
where, for example,

Hungary serves as a hub for German
car companies,

especially during their transition
to the electric vehicle era.

So he's brought in a lot of Asian,
Chinese, South Korean

battery makers
in order to boost Hungary's economy.

It hasn't really delivered
because the transition

to electric vehicles has gone
slightly more slowly than we expected.

And because the German economy
on which Hungary relies
for exports is itself struggling.

Then there were a series of scandals
coming out of the battery factories

relating to leaks of toxic chemicals
into the environment.

At the same time, anger
grew over the widening gap

between a well-connected
elite and ordinary Hungarians.

Corruption
has been one of the biggest problems

under the Orban administration,
according to Transparency International,

Hungary has plunged in rankings
all the way to last place in the European
Union.

Taken together, a stagnant
economy, failing services
and deep corruption created a powder keg.
That opening was seized by Peter Magyar,
a former insider of Orban's ruling elite.
It took an insider turned critic

to credibly deliver
the message that the system is rotten.

Using the once marginal Tisza party
as his vehicle,

Magyar largely
sidestepped Orban's media machine.

He's visited almost every city,
every hamlet.

He's walked hundreds of kilometers
to meet voters.

The new opposition party managed
to not only win over the supporters

of old opposition parties from the left
and the liberals in the capital,
but also build a strong base all over the country.

His appeal is simple keep what voters
like, strip out the corruption,

fix public services and repair Hungary's
relationship with the EU.
Magyar must thread a very small needle

appealing to disillusioned Fidesz voters
without alienating the center.
If you thought Fidesz
that was the right kind of thing,

then you probably are going to think
his party Tisza

is kind of okay as well.
If he is the main receptacle

for just about anybody
who wants to get rid of Orban.
So, faced with a real challenger, Orban

returned to a familiar playbook,
defining a new enemy.
The campaign strategy of Fidesz
has been built on fearmongering.
It was before the center left

and the liberal opposition, Brussels
and EU institutions,
George Soros, migrants, LGBTQ
communities and now it's the war.

Orban has made Ukraine essentially
the centerpiece of his campaign.

There are some really eyecatching posters
up around the country.

The message is that life
in Hungary is tough, sure,
but it could be so much worse.

And these cheeky Ukrainians
are prepared to risk all of that.

Orban is also portraying himself
as a strong leader
who is taken seriously by global leaders.

But it less and less resonates
across the society.

Even if Orban,
Fidesz, loses this election.

It doesn't mean that Fidesz
would lose power in the system.

He will still be the top of a party
with immense power, lots of money,

control of media assets and a demonstrated
will to power essentially.

There are captured state institutions
which could continue to represent
Fidesz’s interests and could significantly
block the actions of a new government.

Orban is the last politician
that anybody would count out.
